channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Mosquito spraying backfires as 84% develop resistance in US neighbourhoods
item.description: New research indicates mosquito spraying is causing insect resistance. Genetic markers for resistance are now widespread across many residential blocks. This resistance is more prevalent in wealthier neighborhoods where spraying occurs frequently. Overuse of chemicals is making insecticides less effective over time. Integrated pest management offers a more sustainable solution for mosquito control.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Middle East crisis, South China Sea tensions dominate ASEAN top diplomats meetings
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Tower of London will open 25,000 architectural drawings to the public for the first time
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: American oystercatchers were wiped out in New England, now their numbers are 45% higher along US coasts; here's how
item.description: Once considered extinct in New England, the American oystercatcher is experiencing a remarkable resurgence thanks to dedicated conservation efforts. These initiatives focus on safeguarding their nesting environments and raising community awareness. Techniques such as banding chicks and preventing disturbances in ground-nesting sites have proven successful. Their resilience, coupled with federal safety measures for related shorebirds, makes their comeback a testament to effective conservation in the face of climate change challenges.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Meet the 73-year-old baby cuddler from Virginia: He has comforted more than 500 premature babies and whispers, 'Grow strong, grow smart, grow kind' before leaving each one
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Who is Kanishka Narayan, Bihar-born MP picked as UK's AI minister
Farmer’s field in Devon has uncovered one of Roman Britain’s hidden treasures
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Florida confirms Burmese python breeding hotspot outside the Everglades, and wildlife experts fear it could be the start of a bigger spread
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Pollution drains energy from Hawaii corals, worsening survival in warming seas
item.description: Human chemicals are accumulating inside coral tissues, weakening their ability to cope. Contaminants from various sources build up, reducing vital nutrient and energy reserves. This internal chemical shift makes corals significantly less resilient to environmental stressors. Monitoring these internal chemical fingerprints offers a new way to track hidden impacts. Reducing chemical pollution is crucial for protecting marine and human health.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Rare Woodhouse's Toad returns after 65 years, caught calling for a mate
item.description: A rare Woodhouse's toad has been spotted at Las Vegas' Springs Preserve for the first time in nearly 65 years. The male was recorded making a mating call in the restored wetland, marking the species' first confirmed return since the original springs dried up around 1962. Conservationists have called the sighting an important moment for local wildlife, while the public has joined in by suggesting names for the lone visitor.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Bill Koch's magnificent Aspen estate, originally listed at $125 million, now draws buyers with $33.515 million price tag
item.description: Billionaire Bill Koch's expansive Aspen estate has sold for $33.515 million after a luxury auction. The property, initially listed at $125 million, struggled to find a buyer for two years. After several price reductions, the 52-acre Elk Mountain Lodge went to a competitive bidding process. This sale highlights shifts in the ultra-luxury housing market and buyer selectivity. The transaction represents a notable luxury home sale in Aspen this year.
item.dc:creator: TOI Real Estate Desk

item.title: Significant rock formation: 2.56 billion-year-old Pichhore granite discovered; offers clues to ancient geological processes
item.description: India's Pichhore orbicular granite, 2.56 billion years old, is a rare geological marvel. This ancient rock formation displays unusual spherical structures called orbicules, which are rarely found globally. Researchers discovered inherited zircon crystals indicating Earth's earliest crust recycled during the Neoarchean Era. The granite's formation suggests magma intrusion and rapid cooling deep beneath the surface. This significant find helps reconstruct conditions of the young Earth and early continents.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Underwater drone found a coral reef written off in the 1960s still alive
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Ancient Greek theatres had wooden stages rolled in and out for every performance
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Landlord firm fined £42,500 after tenants found living among rats and human waste
item.description: A landlord company in County Durham has been fined £42,500 after tenants were found living in unsafe conditions, including sleeping among dead rats, mice, human waste and urine. The court heard the firm failed to carry out required property inspections and maintain valid gas safety certificates across several rental homes, prompting legal action by Durham County Council.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Why people slept inside wooden cupboard beds for hundreds of years in medieval Europe
item.description: Box beds served as enclosed sleeping spaces in European homes for centuries. These wooden structures provided warmth during cold winters and saved limited living space. Families and workers shared these beds, sometimes leading to overcrowding concerns. As housing improved, box beds gradually disappeared from modern households. Modern versions now focus on privacy and design rather than necessity.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: West London streets flooded annually with heavy rains: Five little beavers solved the problem for free by building an extensive dam network
item.description: Ealing borough in London has seen a successful return of beavers, who are tackling flooding issues head-on. By creating wetlands, these industrious animals have managed to retain excess rainwater during intense storms. Remarkably, the area surrounding Greenford station has remained safeguarded from flooding for three years. Additionally, this initiative has revitalized local ecosystems, drawing in diverse wildlife and exemplifying how natural interventions can solve pressing environmental problems.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: A 1,740-year-old Roman coin was found during an English railway dig, revealing a hidden world beneath the ground
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: As temperatures soar, dozens of donkeys in UK are wearing pyjamas and 'don-gerees' to stop painful insect bites, and the clever reason is winning hearts
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: The largest male great white shark ever tagged in the Atlantic has resurfaced after travelling 7,000 miles; scientists say his journey is far from over
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
